column1: Problems include {{c1::constraints::limitations}}, which are unbreakable rules about the problem or how it must be solved.
For programmers, problem solving is defined as writing an original program that performs specific tasks and meets all stated {{c1::constraints}}.
A program that appears to produce correct results but breaks one or more stated rules is called a {{c1::Kobayashi Maru}}.
An {{c1::analogy}} is an exploitable similarity between a solved problem and an unsolved problem.
If a problem solver is unaware of all possible {{c1::operations::actions/moves}}, they may be unable to find a solution.
Gaining new insight into a puzzle can often be achieved by {{c1::restating::rephrasing}} the problem in a more formal manner.
When a complete solution path is unclear, it is better to develop a {{c1::strategy}} than to attack the problem through random trial and error.
When faced with an onerous issue, experimenting with a {{c1::reduced::smaller/simplified}} version of the problem can produce valuable insights.
To make the most immediate progress on a complex task, you should start by evaluating the {{c1::most constrained}} part of the problem.
The most important general rule of problem solving is that you must always have a {{c1::plan}}, rather than engaging in directionless activity.
You can often lower the difficulty of a task by an order of magnitude by finding a way to {{c1::divide}} the problem into separate steps or phases.
When programming, you can build confidence and momentum by starting with what you {{c1::already know}} how to do and working outward.
You can narrow the scope of an unsolvable situation by adding or removing {{c1::constraints::rules}} to produce a problem you do know how to solve.
Unlike random guessing, an {{c1::experiment}} is a controlled process where you test a hypothesis and observe the results.
Because a programmer's anger is internal rather than an external stimulus, avoiding {{c1::frustration}} is a conscious decision you must make.
